---
title: "Introduction to Biostatistics - Learn Statistics the Right Way!"
subtitle: "Descriptive Statistics"
author: Justin Belair | Statistical Consultant | Author of Causal Inference in Statistics
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "metropolis"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "structurebold"
    highlight: "tango"
    incremental: true
    fig_width: 7
    fig_height: 5
    fig_caption: true
    df_print: "kable"
    latex_engine: "xelatex"
    #keep_tex: true
    slide_level: 2
aspectratio: "169"
classoption: "professionalfonts"
header-includes:
  - \hypersetup{colorlinks=true, linkcolor=blue, urlcolor=blue, pdfnewwindow=true}
  - \definecolor{dark_blue}{RGB}{29,42,59}
  - \definecolor{light_blue}{RGB}{227,237,255}
  - \setbeamercolor{frametitle}{bg=dark_blue, fg=light_blue}
  - \setbeamercolor{title}{fg=dark_blue}
  - \setbeamercolor{structure}{fg=dark_blue}
  - \usepackage{tikz}
  - \addtobeamertemplate{footline}{}{
      \begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture,overlay]
      \node[anchor=south west,yshift=-22pt,xshift=-5pt] at (current page.south west) {
        \href{https://justinbelair.ca/?utm_source=intro_biostats&utm_medium=slides&utm_campaign=logo_click}{\includegraphics[height=2.5cm]{C:/Users/justi/Documents/JBConsulting/Fiverr/Logo/JB_Statistical_Consulting_no_calligraphy_black.png}}
      };
      \end{tikzpicture}
    }
---

## Describing Distributions With Summary Statistics - The Median

- The *median* is another measure of central tendency
- It is the value that *separates* the variables's data into two equal parts, after it has been put in increasing order
  - It is calculated by ordering the data points and selecting the middle value
  - If the number of data points is even, the median is the average of the two middle values.
  
## Describing Distributions With Summary Statistics - The Median
- Consider again our example with the salary data
  - Ordering the data points, we have 1000, 1300, 1500, 2000, 50000
  - The median is the middle value, that is 1500 dollars
  - This value fits better with our intuition of a "typical" salary than the mean, which was influenced by an outlier

## Describing Distributions With Summary Statistics - The Median (Coding Example)

- Of course, when there are many data points, it is not practical to compute the median by hand and the task is best left to software

```{r}
salary <- c(1000, 2000, 1500, 1300, 50000)

salary_median <- median(salary)
salary_median
```
## Describing Distributions With Summary Statistics - The Median (Coding Example)

```{python}
import numpy as np

salary = [1000, 2000, 1500, 1300, 50000]

salary_median = np.median(salary)
print(salary_median)
```

- Voilà!

## Describing Distributions With Summary Statistics - The Median

- The median is considered a *robust* measure of central tendency
- The word *robust* has a special meaning in statistics
- It means that a certain procedure, estimator, or approach is known to perform well even when we are faced with data that has certain *undesirable* or *problematic* features 
- It this case, the problematic feature of the data was the presence of an outlier
- The median is more *robust* to these outliers than the mean as a measure of central tendency 
- It is often used when the data is *skewed* or has outliers

## Describing Distributions With Summary Statistics - The Median

- We can also come up with examples where the median is influenced by undesirable features of a variable's distribution
- Consider the following example
  - We measure the weekly salary of 6 individuals in dollars: 1300, 5000, 1600, 1500, 1400, 10000
  - The median is calculated as the average of the middle values of the ordered data points, which is $(1500+1600)/2 = 1550$  dollars
- Now, consider a small change in the data set
  - We measure the weekly salary of 6 individuals in dollars: 1300, 5000, 1600, 15000, 1400, 10000
  - The median is calculated as the middle value of the ordered data points, which is $(1600+5000)/2 = 3300$ dollars
  - By changing one data point, the median more than doubled!
  
## Takeaways

- A statistic is *robust* to certain features - it cannot be robust to everything!
- The mean is robust to changes in the middle of the distribution, the median is robust to outliers
- Keep this in mind: in statistics, nothing is perfect!
- We use different tools to answer different questions in different settings  
  
## Contact Information and other clickable stuff

\small
- [**My Personal Website**](https://justinbelair.ca?utm_source=intro_biostats&utm_medium=slides_median&utm_campaign=contact_info)
- If you are interested in Consulting, Training, Workshops, Public Speaking, Guest Lectures, and Other Custom Services in statistics and causal inference, please do not hesitate to contact me **belairjustin@gmail.com**
- The first chapter of my book, *Causal Inference in Statistics*, is available for free on my website by [**clicking here**](https://justinbelair.ca/causal-inference-in-statistics-book/?utm_source=intro_biostats&utm_medium=slides_median&utm_campaign=contact_info)
- I share a montly newsletter to stats nerds, causal inference enthusiasts, and data scientists, [**subscribe here**](https://causal-inference-in-statistics.beehiiv.com/subscribe?utm_source=intro_biostats&utm_medium=slides_median&utm_campaign=contact_info)
- I founded [**biostatistics.ca**](https://www.biostatistics.ca/?utm_source=intro_biostats&utm_medium=slides_median&utm_campaign=contact_info), a blog and biostatistics community.
- I share content daily on [**Linkedin**](https://www.linkedin.com/in/justinbelair/)
- I created a [**Linkedin Causal Inference Group**](https://www.linkedin.com/groups/13065272/) with [**Aleksander** Molak](https://www.linkedin.com/in/aleksandermolak/)
- I created a [**Causal Inference Discord Community**](https://discord.com/invite/2xKvHMhYDg) which is free to join
